title: Sorting
description: Sorting is fundamental to interview algorithms — from binary search prerequisites to greedy approaches. Master TypeScript's typed comparators to sort numbers, strings, and objects correctly.

sections.title: Numeric sort with typed comparator
sections.explanation: JavaScript's default `.sort()` converts elements to strings, so `[10, 2, 1].sort()` gives `[1, 10, 2]`. In interviews, always pass a numeric comparator: `(a, b) => a - b` for ascending, `(a, b) => b - a` for descending. TypeScript infers the comparator types from the array.

exercise.prompt: Sort the array [30, 5, 20, 1, 15] in ascending order and print it joined by comma. Then sort [30, 5, 20, 1, 15] in descending order and print it joined by comma.
exercise.starterCode:
const asc: number[] = [30, 5, 20, 1, 15]
asc.sort((a, b) => a ___ b)
console.log(asc.join(","))

const desc: number[] = [30, 5, 20, 1, 15]
desc.sort((a, b) => b ___ a)
console.log(desc.join(","))

validation.message: Use a - b for ascending and b - a for descending

exercise.hint: The comparator a - b sorts ascending (negative means a comes first). b - a reverses the order.

sections.title: Sort by object property
sections.explanation: Interviews often require sorting objects by a specific field — like sorting intervals by start time or events by priority. Define an interface for the object type, then use a comparator accessing the property.

exercise.prompt: Define an interface `Student` with `name: string` and `grade: number`. Sort an array of students by grade descending. Print each student as "name: grade".

validation.message: Define Student interface and sort by grade descending

exercise.hint: For descending order by grade, use b.grade - a.grade in the comparator.

sections.title: Multi-key sort
sections.explanation: Sometimes you need to sort by a primary key, breaking ties with a secondary key. The comparator checks the primary key first — if equal (returns 0), it falls through to the secondary key. This pattern appears in scheduling, ranking, and ordering problems.

exercise.prompt: Sort an array of [score, name] pairs: first by score descending, then by name ascending for ties. Print each as "name: score".

validation.message: Use b-a for descending score and localeCompare for name

exercise.hint: For descending score: b[0] - a[0]. For ascending name on ties: a[1].localeCompare(b[1]).

sections.title: Custom sort for interview patterns
sections.explanation: Several interview patterns rely on sorting as a preprocessing step: sorting intervals by start, sorting characters by frequency, or sorting to enable two-pointer approaches. Creating a sorted copy with `[...arr].sort()` preserves the original — important when you need the original indices.

exercise.prompt: Write a function that takes a string and returns its characters sorted by frequency (most frequent first). For ties, use alphabetical order. Print the result for "tree".

validation.message: e appears twice (first), then r and t once each (alphabetical)
Count frequencies and sort descending by count

exercise.hint: Sort by frequency descending: b[1] - a[1]. For ties, alphabetical: a[0].localeCompare(b[0]). Repeat each char by its count.
